name: Offences and the millstone
notes: A threefold synoptic on the gravity of causing others to stumble, joining Matthew, Mark, and Luke. All three share the millstone saying: better a millstone about the neck and drowning in the sea than to offend one of these little ones which believe in Christ. Matthew and Luke together pronounce the woe over offences that must needs come, while Matthew and Mark together press the call to cut off the hand or foot, or pluck out the eye, that offends, rather than be cast into hell fire. Each Evangelist keeps his own vantage: Luke sets the millstone after the warning as the lesser evil to offending, Matthew gathers hand and foot into one verse where Mark unfolds them separately, and Mark alone triples the solemn refrain, 'Where their worm dieth not, and the fire is not quenched,' which reads in place. The differing arrangement of the same dominical words is left as a matter for further study.
